description: This figure presents a comprehensive quantitative analysis of strengthening mechanisms in Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C low-density steels with varying aluminum content (2.59–11.58 wt%). The image displays a stacked bar chart decomposing the yield strength contributions from base strength, solid solution strengthening by Al, grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch), ferrite phase strengthening, and κ-carbide precipitation hardening across 3Al, 6Al, 10Al, and 12Al alloys. All samples underwent identical thermomechanical processing: hot rolling to 3 mm thickness with 85% reduction, solution treatment at 1100°C for 1 hour, and water quenching. The caption states that yield strength increases from 321 MPa (3Al) to 952 MPa (12Al), with the 12Al steel exhibiting a κ-carbide precipitation strengthening contribution of approximately 361.93 MPa—1.78 times the solid solution hardening effect. The figure illustrates the microstructural evolution from single-phase austenite (80 μm grain size) in 3Al and 6Al steels to a triplex structure in 12Al steel containing 7 vol% ferrite and refined 40 μm austenite grains with coherent nanosized κ-carbides, demonstrating synergistic multi-mechanism strengthening.

This figure presents a comprehensive microstructural characterization of Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C low-density steels (x = 3, 6, 10, 12 wt% Al) after solution treatment at 1100°C for 1 hour and water quenching. Panels (a)-(d) display optical micrographs revealing the grain structure evolution with increasing Al content: 3Al and 6Al steels exhibit fully austenitic microstructures with similar grain sizes (~80 μm excluding twin boundaries), while 10Al steel shows austenite with extensive annealing twins, and 12Al steel contains approximately 7 vol% ferrite (appearing as small white blocky phase) with refined austenite grains (~40 μm, or ~16 μm including twin boundaries). Panels (e) and (f) show TEM bright-field images and corresponding selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns for 10Al and 12Al steels, respectively. The SAED pattern in (e) exhibits weak superlattice spots indicating the presence of ordered (Fe,Mn)3AlCx (κ) carbides in 10Al steel, whereas (f) displays strong superlattice spots and two sets of modulation contrast in 12Al steel, evidencing extensive κ-carbide precipitation even in the as-quenched condition. These observations establish that increasing Al content promotes κ-carbide formation when exceeding 10 wt%, contributing significantly to the dramatic yield strength increase from 470 MPa (10Al) to 952 MPa (12Al) through precipitation strengthening.

The figure presents mechanical property characterization of Fe-26Mn-6Al-1C (6Al) steel to investigate the grain size effect on strengthening. Subfigure (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves for specimens solution-treated at three temperatures: 1000°C (blue), 1100°C (red), and 1200°C (black). As solution temperature decreases, both yield strength and ultimate tensile strength increase significantly while elongation reduces, indicating grain refinement strengthening. Subfigure (b) plots yield strength versus the inverse square root of grain size (d^-1/2), showing a linear Hall-Petch relationship. Experimental data points at d^-1/2 ≈ 0.14, 0.20, and 0.26 μm^-1/2 correspond to yield strengths of approximately 340 MPa, 378 MPa, and 447 MPa respectively, with a linear fit confirming the Hall-Petch behavior. The grain size range covered is approximately 5.38–52 μm. These results quantitatively demonstrate that grain boundary strengthening contributes significantly to the overall yield strength in 6Al steel.

This stacked bar chart quantifies the individual contributions of five distinct strengthening mechanisms to the total yield strength of Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C low-density steels across varying aluminum contents (3Al, 6Al, 10Al, and 12Al, corresponding to approximately 2.6–11.6 wt% Al). The y-axis displays yield strength in MPa, ranging from 0 to 1000, while each bar is segmented to represent Base strength, Solid solution by Al, Ferrite, Grain refining, and Carbide (κ-carbide) precipitation. The chart reveals a monotonic increase in total yield strength from approximately 320 MPa for 3Al to roughly 950 MPa for 12Al. While the 3Al and 6Al alloys derive strength primarily from base properties and solid solution hardening, the 12Al alloy exhibits a dramatic enhancement dominated by Carbide precipitation (approximately 360 MPa contribution), accompanied by significant contributions from Grain refining and Ferrite phases. This visualization substantiates the conclusion that κ-carbide precipitation becomes the predominant strengthening mechanism at higher aluminum contents, accounting for the exceptional yield strength in the 12Al composition compared to the modest gains from solid solution alone.
